input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The system under study is a **1/10-scale autonomous vehicle** retrofitted for adaptive cruise control (ACC) functionality. The physical twin (PT) consists of a **Traxxas Slash 4x4 Premium** base chassis (lower level) and an **upper level chassis** hosting autonomy components, including an **NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano** microcomputer, **NVIDIA Jetson NX antenna**, **Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR**, **Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera**, and **Vedder Electronic Speed Controller**. The vehicle operates under **ROS2 middleware** and **NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK**, enabling real-time sensor fusion and autonomous control. The PT is designed to simulate an **ego vehicle** in an ACC system, interacting with a **lead vehicle** via sensor data and actuation commands. The system adheres to **ISO 15622** for ACC functionality (performance class I) and follows the **RoboRacer open-source project** for hardware retrofitting.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The physical acting components of the DT constellation include:
    - **Motor Actuation**: Converts desired speed commands into motor voltage commands via the **Vedder Electronic Speed Controller**, interfacing with the vehicle's lower-level chassis.
    - **Steering Servo Actuation**: Controls the steering angle of the vehicle, integrated into the lower-level chassis.
    - **Emergency Braking**: Implemented via the motor actuation system, triggered by remote commands from the DT.
    These components are controlled by the **Actuation Functional Entity (FE)** in the **Physical Twin Entity**, which translates high-level commands (e.g., desired speed, braking) into low-level motor/servo signals. The actuation is executed on the **NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano** using **ROS2 nodes** for real-time control.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The physical sensing components include:
    - **Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR**: Captures 3D point clouds of the vehicle's surroundings, enabling object detection and tracking.
    - **Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera**: Provides RGB-D data for environmental perception and object recognition.
    - **Vehicle State Sensors**: Measure real-time parameters such as **ego vehicle speed** and **steering angle**, integrated into the lower-level chassis.
    - **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking**: Implemented via **DBSCAN clustering** and **Kalman filtering** (using **MATLAB/Simulink** and **Python 3.8**) to generate **object tracks** (e.g., lead vehicle detection) from raw LiDAR data.
    These sensors feed data into the **Data Pre-Processing FE** and **Data Collection FE** of the **Physical Twin Entity**, which preprocess and transmit data to the **Digital Twin Entity** for further processing.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The physical-to-virtual interaction involves the following data streams and events:
    - **Real-time sensor data**: LiDAR point clouds, camera feeds, and vehicle state (speed, steering angle) are transmitted from the **Physical Twin Entity** to the **Digital Twin Entity** via **ROS2 middleware** and **Wi-Fi network** (using **UDP/TCP protocols**).
    - **Object tracks**: Processed via **DBSCAN clustering** and **Kalman filtering** in the **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** component, generating structured data (e.g., lead vehicle position, velocity) for the **Data Collection FE**.
    - **Vehicle state data**: Includes **ego vehicle speed**, **steering angle**, and **detected objects** (e.g., lead vehicle), collected at a frequency aligned with the **100 ms latency requirement** (R7).
    - **Data translation**: The **Data Translation FE** serializes and aligns message types (e.g., converting ROS2 topics to standardized formats) for inter-entity communication. Unit conversions (e.g., LiDAR coordinates to vehicle-centric frames) are performed to ensure compatibility between the **Physical Twin Entity** and **Digital Twin Entity**.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The virtual-to-physical interaction includes:
    - **Desired speed commands**: Computed by the **ACC Controlling FE** in the **Digital Twin Entity**, sent back to the **Actuation FE** in the **Physical Twin Entity** via **ROS2** and **Wi-Fi**.
    - **Remote management commands**: Enabled through the **User Entity**, including:
        - **Enable/Disable ACC**: Triggers activation/deactivation of the ACC system.
        - **Modify desired speed**: Adjusts the target speed for the ego vehicle.
        - **Emergency braking**: Overrides normal operation to halt the vehicle immediately.
    - **Actuation commands**: Translated into **motor voltage commands** (via **Actuation FE**) and **steering servo signals**, executed on the **NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano** using **ROS2 node lifecycle management**.
    - **Latency constraints**: The system enforces a **maximum 100 ms end-to-end latency** (R7) for virtual-to-physical commands to ensure real-time responsiveness, adhering to **3GPP TS 22.185** for V2V communication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin system provides the following services:
    1. **Data Monitoring**:
       - Real-time visualization of **ego vehicle state** (speed, steering angle), **object tracks** (e.g., lead vehicle), and **projected driving path** via a **3D Visualization UI**.
       - **Report generation**: Creates human-readable timeseries reports of vehicle state and object tracks using the **Reporting FE**.
       - **Data storage**: Stores ego vehicle state and object tracks in an **external SQL database** (via **Data Storage** component) for post-processing and analysis.
    2. **Computation Offloading**:
       - Offloads **ACC computation** (lead vehicle detection, speed control) from the resource-constrained **NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano** to the **Digital Twin Entity**, reducing on-board computational load.
       - Enables **cloud-based processing** for complex tasks (e.g., point cloud clustering) while maintaining real-time performance.
    3. **Remote Management**:
       - Provides a **Control Panel UI** for remote enabling/disabling of ACC, setting desired speed, and issuing emergency braking commands.
       - Supports **geographically distributed operation**, allowing developers/operators to manage the system remotely.
    These services are implemented using **ROS2 nodes**, **Python 3.8**, and **MATLAB/Simulink**, with data processed in the **Digital Twin Entity** and presented via the **User Entity**.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale:
The twinning time scale is **real-time** with the following constraints:
    - **Data collection and processing**: Sensor data (LiDAR, camera, vehicle state) is collected and processed at a frequency sufficient to meet the **100 ms latency requirement** (R7) for end-to-end communication.
    - **ACC control loop**: The **Controlling FE** computes desired speed and sends commands to the physical twin with a cycle time aligned to the **100 ms synchronization accuracy** (R8) for clock synchronization.
    - **Simulation and experimentation**: The DT supports **cyber-physical experimentation** during development, with real-time interaction between the virtual and physical environments. For example, **purposeful experimentation** (e.g., testing ACC responses to lead vehicle maneuvers) is enabled by the **Collection Identification FE** and **User Entity**.
    - **Offline analysis**: While real-time operations are prioritized, the **Data Storage** component allows for post-hoc analysis of stored data (e.g., performance metrics, fault diagnosis) at a lower temporal resolution.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities:
The DT system is implemented in a **centralized yet modular architecture**, with the following multiplicities:
    - **Single Digital Twin Entity**: Hosts core DT functionality (e.g., **ACC computation**, **data collection**, **reporting**) and is deployed on a **cloud or edge server** for computation offloading.
    - **Single Physical Twin Entity**: Represents the **1/10-scale autonomous vehicle**, hosting **sensors**, **actuators**, and **pre-processing** components (e.g., **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking**).
    - **Single User Entity**: Provides a unified **3D Visualization** and **Control Panel** interface for operators and developers.
    - **Decentralized deployment**: Components are distributed across hardware:
        - **Physical Twin Entity**: Deployed on the **NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano** (on-board the vehicle).
        - **Digital Twin Entity**: Deployed on an **external server** (cloud/edge) for computation offloading.
        - **User Entity**: Accessible via a **remote UI** (e.g., web-based or desktop application).
    - **Functional Entity (FE) granularity**: The system adheres to the **ISO 23247 RA** but introduces deviations, such as the **Physical Twin Entity** (not defined in the standard) and the **splitting of the User Interface FE** into **3D Visualization** and **Control Panel** subcomponents. This modularity allows for **scalable extensions** (e.g., adding new sensors or actuators).
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The DT operates across the following lifecycle stages of the physical system:
    1. **Design Phase**:
       - Used as a **cyber-physical experimentation facility** for validating ACC algorithms and system requirements (e.g., **ISO 15622 compliance**).
       - Enables **3D CAD integration** (via **3D Visualization**) for virtual prototyping and simulation.
    2. **Development Phase**:
       - Supports **rapid prototyping** (using **Python 3.8** and **ROS2**) and **troubleshooting** via real-time data monitoring.
       - Facilitates **requirements validation** (e.g., latency, clock synchronization) through empirical testing.
    3. **Operation Phase**:
       - Provides **real-time health monitoring** (e.g., vehicle performance, sensor faults) and **predictive maintenance** (e.g., brake system prognosis).
       - Enables **remote management** of ACC during operation, including **emergency interventions**.
    4. **End-of-Life Phase**:
       - Supports **product lifecycle management** by maintaining a **digital thread** of vehicle data (e.g., historical performance metrics, maintenance logs).
    The DT covers the **as-operated** representation phase, with a focus on **real-time synchronization** between the virtual and physical systems. The **fidelity** of the DT is high for **ACC-relevant data** (e.g., speed, steering, object tracks) but may vary for other subsystems not explicitly modeled.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The DT incorporates the following models and data:
    - **Sensor Models**:
        - **LiDAR Point Cloud Model**: Raw data from **Hokuyo UST-10LX** is processed using **DBSCAN clustering** and **Kalman filtering** to generate **object tracks**.
        - **Camera Model**: **Intel Deepsense D435i** provides RGB-D data for environmental context, integrated with LiDAR data for robust object detection.
    - **Control Models**:
        - **ACC Control Model**: Implements **ISO 15622 performance class I** (basic speed control without road curvature), using **PID-like control strategies** for desired speed computation.
        - **Actuation Model**: Translates virtual commands (e.g., desired speed) into **motor voltage** and **steering servo signals** via the **Actuation FE**.
    - **Data**:
        - **Real-time vehicle state**: Includes **speed**, **steering angle**, and **detected objects** (e.g., lead vehicle).
        - **Historical data**: Stored in an **external SQL database** for post-processing, reporting, and lifecycle analysis.
        - **Simulated data**: Used for **AI agent training** (via **User Entity integration**) and **purposeful experimentation**.
    - **Visualization Models**:
        - **3D CAD Model**: Renders the vehicle and its environment in real-time, synchronized with sensor data.
        - **Projected Driving Path**: Computed by the **Digital Twin Entity** and displayed in the **3D Visualization UI**.
    The models are validated through **empirical testing** (e.g., comparing virtual predictions with physical outcomes) and **requirements compliance** (e.g., latency, accuracy).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The DT leverages the following tools and enablers:
    - **Middleware**:
        - **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)**: Enables modular communication between **ROS2 nodes** (e.g., sensor drivers, control algorithms) and inter-entity data exchange.
        - **NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK**: Provides hardware acceleration for **Jetson Orin Nano** and **Jetson NX antenna**, supporting real-time sensor processing.
    - **Programming Languages**:
        - **Python 3.8**: Used for rapid prototyping (e.g., **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking**).
        - **C++**: Implements **ROS2 node lifecycle management** for critical components (e.g., actuation).
        - **MATLAB/Simulink**: Used for complex algorithms (e.g., **Kalman filtering**, **control logic**).
    - **Data Processing**:
        - **DBSCAN**: For **LiDAR point cloud clustering**.
        - **Kalman Filter**: For **object tracking** and state estimation.
    - **Communication Protocols**:
        - **Wi-Fi (UDP/TCP)**: For inter-entity communication between **Physical Twin Entity** and **Digital Twin Entity**.
        - **ROS2 Topics**: For intra-entity communication (e.g., sensor data to control algorithms).
    - **Visualization Tools**:
        - **3D Visualization UI**: Built using **ROS2 RViz** or custom **OpenGL/WebGL** renderers for real-time display.
    - **Cloud/Edge Enablers**:
        - **Computation offloading**: Enables **cloud-based processing** for resource-intensive tasks (e.g., ACC computation) while maintaining **<100 ms latency**.
    - **Standardized Interfaces**:
        - **OPC UA (potential)**: Mentioned in ISO 23247 as an option for **Data Translation FE**, though not explicitly implemented in this case study.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The DT constellation is organized as follows:
    - **Entities**:
        1. **Physical Twin Entity**:
           - Hosts **sensors** (LiDAR, camera, vehicle state sensors) and **actuators** (motor, steering servo).
           - Implements **Data Pre-Processing FE** (e.g., point cloud clustering) and **Actuation FE**.
           - Deployed on the **NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano** on-board the vehicle.
        2. **Digital Twin Entity**:
           - Hosts **core DT logic**, including **ACC computation**, **Data Collection FE**, **Reporting FE**, and **Data Storage**.
           - Deployed on an **external server** (cloud/edge) for computation offloading.
        3. **User Entity**:
           - Provides **3D Visualization** and **Control Panel** interfaces for operators/developers.
           - Accessible via a **remote UI** (e.g., web or desktop application).
    - **Functional Entities (FEs)**:
        - **Data Translation FE**: Serializes and aligns data between entities (e.g., ROS2 topics to standardized formats).
        - **Presentation FE**: Pre-processes data for visualization (e.g., converting sensor data to 3D renderable formats).
        - **Controlling FE**: Implements ACC logic (e.g., lead vehicle detection, speed control).
        - **Reporting FE**: Generates human-readable reports from stored data.
    - **Deployment Architecture**:
        - **Centralized DT logic** with **modular components**, allowing for **scalable extensions** (e.g., adding new sensors or actuators).
        - **Decoupled entities** communicate via **ROS2 middleware** (intra-entity) and **Wi-Fi** (inter-entity), adhering to **ISO 23247 RA** with adaptations for automotive use.
    - **Data Flow**:
        - Sensor data → **Physical Twin Entity** → **Digital Twin Entity** → **User Entity** (for visualization/control).
        - Commands → **User Entity** → **Digital Twin Entity** → **Physical Twin Entity** (for actuation).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process and DT evolution follow these stages:
    1. **Requirements Definition**:
       - Derived from **case-based generalization** of automotive projects, focusing on **real-time constraints** (e.g., latency, clock synchronization) and **safety-critical operations** (e.g., ACC).
       - Key requirements include **standardized communication** (R1), **real-time data collection** (R2), **external data storage** (R3), **UI transparency** (R4), **remote management** (R5), **ACC compliance** (R6), **latency constraints** (R7), and **clock synchronization** (R8).
    2. **Architectural Design**:
       - Based on **ISO 23247 RA**, with adaptations for automotive use (e.g., introducing the **Physical Twin Entity**).
       - Components mapped to **ROS2 packages** for modularity and loose coupling.
    3. **Implementation**:
       - **Physical Twin Entity**: Implemented on the **Jetson Orin Nano** using **ROS2**, **Python**, and **MATLAB/Simulink**.
       - **Digital Twin Entity**: Deployed on an **external server** for computation offloading.
       - **User Entity**: Built as a **remote UI** with **3D visualization** and **control panel** functionalities.
    4. **Integration and Testing**:
       - **Empirical validation** of the DT through real-world operation of the **1/10-scale autonomous vehicle**.
       - Testing focuses on **latency**, **clock synchronization**, and **ACC performance** (e.g., following distance maintenance).
    5. **Quality Assurance**:
       - **Verification**: Ensures compliance with **ISO 15622** (ACC) and **3GPP TS 22.185** (latency).
       - **Validation**: Conducted via **real-time experimentation** (e.g., testing ACC responses to lead vehicle maneuvers).
    6. **Evolution**:
       - Future upgrades may include:
         - **Integration with other ADAS systems** (e.g., lane-keeping assist).
         - **Enhanced simulation capabilities** (e.g., physics-based models for higher fidelity).
         - **Automated testing frameworks** for continuous validation.
       - The **modular architecture** allows for incremental updates without redesigning the entire system.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
Fidelity and validity are ensured through:
    - **Real-time synchronization**:
        - **100 ms latency requirement** (R7) and **100 ms clock synchronization** (R8) ensure temporal alignment between the virtual and physical systems.
        - **ROS2 middleware** provides deterministic communication for critical data streams (e.g., sensor inputs, actuation commands).
    - **Model validation**:
        - **ACC control model** is validated against **ISO 15622** (performance class I) requirements.
        - **Object tracking** is validated via **Kalman filter accuracy** and **DBSCAN clustering performance** (e.g., false positive/negative rates).
    - **Empirical testing**:
        - The DT is tested in **real-world scenarios** (e.g., dynamic lead vehicle behavior) to validate its predictive and control capabilities.
        - **Purposeful experimentation** (e.g., injecting faults into sensor data) is used to assess robustness.
    - **Uncertainty management**:
        - **Sensor noise** (e.g., LiDAR measurement errors) is mitigated via **Kalman filtering** and **data fusion** (LiDAR + camera).
        - **Actuation delays** are accounted for in the **control loop** to maintain stability.
    - **Verification and validation mechanisms**:
        - **Unit testing**: Individual **ROS2 nodes** and **FEs** are tested in isolation.
        - **Integration testing**: Ensures seamless communication between entities (e.g., **Physical Twin Entity** → **Digital Twin Entity**).
        - **System testing**: Validates end-to-end functionality (e.g., ACC following a lead vehicle).
    - **Fidelity trade-offs**:
        - High fidelity for **ACC-relevant data** (e.g., speed, steering, object tracks) but lower fidelity for non-critical subsystems (e.g., non-ACC sensors).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The technical connection between the PT and DT includes:
    - **Network Protocols**:
        - **Wi-Fi (UDP/TCP)**: Used for inter-entity communication between the **Physical Twin Entity** (on-board) and **Digital Twin Entity** (external server).
        - **ROS2 Topics**: Facilitates intra-entity communication (e.g., sensor data to control algorithms) within the **Physical Twin Entity**.
    - **Architecture**:
        - **Client-Server Model**: The **Digital Twin Entity** acts as a server, receiving sensor data and sending commands to the **Physical Twin Entity** (client).
        - **Pub-Sub Model**: **ROS2** uses a **publish-subscribe** paradigm for decoupled communication between components.
    - **Latency and Synchronization**:
        - **End-to-end latency**: ≤100 ms (R7), adhering to **3GPP TS 22.185** for V2V communication.
        - **Clock synchronization**: Devices synchronized to **±100 ms accuracy** (R8) using **NTP-like protocols** (exact method not specified but implied for distributed systems).
    - **Data Translation**:
        - The **Data Translation FE** handles:
            - **Serialization**: Converts ROS2 messages to standardized formats.
            - **Unit conversion**: Aligns coordinate systems (e.g., LiDAR frames to vehicle-centric frames).
            - **Message alignment**: Ensures compatibility between **Physical Twin Entity** and **Digital Twin Entity** data streams.
    - **Hardware Interfaces**:
        - **NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano**: Acts as the **edge gateway**, processing sensor data and executing actuation commands.
        - **Wi-Fi Module**: Enables wireless communication between the vehicle and external server.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The DT is hosted and deployed as follows:
    - **Physical Twin Entity**:
        - Deployed on the **NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano** (on-board the vehicle), running **ROS2** and **Jetpack 5.0 SDK**.
        - Hosts **sensor drivers**, **pre-processing algorithms** (e.g., point cloud clustering), and **actuation logic**.
    - **Digital Twin Entity**:
        - Deployed on an **external server** (cloud or edge) for computation offloading.
        - Runs **Python 3.8**, **C++ (ROS2 nodes)**, and **MATLAB/Simulink** for core DT logic (e.g., ACC computation, reporting).
        - Uses **SQL database** for storing historical data (e.g., vehicle state, object tracks).
    - **User Entity**:
        - Accessible via a **remote UI** (e.g., web-based or desktop application).
        - Implemented using **ROS2 RViz** or custom **3D rendering tools** (e.g., OpenGL/WebGL).
    - **Deployment Strategy**:
        - **Modular and scalable**: Components are deployed as **independent ROS2 packages**, allowing for incremental updates.
        - **Hybrid cloud-edge**: Combines **on-board processing** (for latency-sensitive tasks) with **cloud processing** (for resource-intensive tasks).
        - **Containerization**: Potential use of **Docker/Kubernetes** (not explicitly mentioned but implied for scalable deployment).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The DT provides the following insights and supports decision-making:
    - **Real-time monitoring**:
        - **Vehicle state visualization**: Displays **speed**, **steering angle**, and **object tracks** in a **3D environment**, enabling operators to assess system health.
        - **ACC performance metrics**: Tracks **following distance**, **speed adjustments**, and **emergency braking events**.
    - **Predictive analytics**:
        - **Fault detection**: Identifies anomalies in sensor data (e.g., LiDAR dropouts) or actuator behavior (e.g., motor stalls).
        - **Predictive maintenance**: Enables **brake system prognosis** (as referenced in related work) by analyzing historical data.
    - **Remote management decisions**:
        - **ACC tuning**: Operators can adjust **desired speed** or **disable ACC** based on real-time conditions (e.g., poor sensor data quality).
        - **Emergency interventions**: Remote **emergency braking** can be triggered via the **Control Panel** in critical scenarios.
    - **Development insights**:
        - **Troubleshooting**: Developers use **real-time data logs** and **reports** to diagnose issues (e.g., tracking errors, control instability).
        - **Algorithm validation**: Compares **virtual predictions** (e.g., ACC commands) with **physical outcomes** to refine models.
    - **Lifecycle decisions**:
        - **Maintenance scheduling**: Historical data informs **predictive maintenance** plans (e.g., brake wear analysis).
        - **System upgrades**: Insights from **real-world operation** guide future DT enhancements (e.g., adding new ADAS features).
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
Horizontal integration involves:
    - **External information systems**:
        - **Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)**: The DT supports **digital threads** by maintaining a continuous flow of vehicle data (e.g., design, manufacturing, operation) across the lifecycle.
        - **Simulation tools**: Integration with **3D CAD systems** (e.g., **CREO Simulate**) for virtual prototyping and validation.
        - **Cloud platforms**: Potential integration with **IoT platforms** (e.g., AWS IoT, Azure Digital Twins) for scalable data storage and analytics.
    - **Standardized interfaces**:
        - **OPC UA**: Mentioned in ISO 23247 as a potential enabler for **data exchange** with external systems (e.g., enterprise ERP or MES).
        - **ROS2**: Supports integration with other **robotics/automotive tools** via its **open-source ecosystem**.
    - **Inter-DT communication**:
        - Not explicitly addressed in the case study, but the **modular architecture** allows for future extensions to support **fleet-wide DT coordination** (e.g., sharing ACC data across multiple vehicles).
    - **Data exchange formats**:
        - **Standardized message formats** (via **Data Translation FE**) enable compatibility with external systems (e.g., converting ROS2 topics to **ISO 23247-compliant** data models).
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy:
Data ownership and privacy considerations include:
    - **Data ownership**:
        - The **physical twin owner** (e.g., the entity operating the 1/10-scale vehicle) retains ownership of the **real-time sensor data** and **vehicle state logs**.
        - The **DT service provider** (e.g., developers of the DTS) may have access to anonymized or aggregated data for **system improvement** but does not own raw data.
    - **Data privacy**:
        - **Sensitive data** (e.g., vehicle location, operator commands) is **not explicitly stored or transmitted** in raw form; only **processed data** (e.g., object tracks, speed) is shared.
        - **Access control**: The **User Entity** enforces role-based access (e.g., operators vs. developers) to ensure only authorized personnel can manage the system.
    - **Compliance**:
        - While not explicitly stated, the system likely adheres to **automotive data privacy standards** (e.g., **ISO/SAE 21434** for cybersecurity) and **GDPR** if deployed in regions with strict data protection laws.
    - **Data retention**:
        - **Historical data** is stored in an **external SQL database**, with retention policies not specified but implied to be **purpose-bound** (e.g., for maintenance or validation).
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The DT adheres to the following standards:
    - **Architectural Standard**:
        - **ISO 23247 (Part 2)**: The **reference architecture** is adapted for automotive use, with deviations such as the **Physical Twin Entity** and **split User Interface FE**.
    - **Automotive Standards**:
        - **ISO 15622**: Defines the **ACC control logic** (performance class I) implemented in the **Controlling FE**.
        - **3GPP TS 22.185**: Specifies the **100 ms latency requirement** for V2V communication.
    - **Communication Standards**:
        - **ROS2**: Used as the **middleware** for intra-entity communication, though not a formal standard for DTs.
        - **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**: Used for inter-entity communication, adhering to general networking standards.
    - **Safety and Cybersecurity**:
        - **ISO/SAE 21434**: Likely followed for **cybersecurity** (though not explicitly mentioned).
        - **Functional Safety**: Implied for **actuation commands** (e.g., emergency braking) to prevent unsafe operations.
    - **Data Standards**:
        - **ISO 23247 Part 4**: For **information exchange** (e.g., data translation between entities).
        - **OPC UA**: Mentioned as a potential standard for **Data Translation FE** (though not implemented in this case study).
    - **Development Standards**:
        - **NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK**: Standardized development environment for **Jetson Orin Nano**.
        - **ROS2 Best Practices**: Followed for **modularity**, **loose coupling**, and **real-time performance**.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
Security and safety considerations include:
    - **Safety Mechanisms**:
        - **Real-time constraints**: Enforced **100 ms latency** (R7) and **100 ms clock synchronization** (R8) to ensure **deterministic behavior** for safety-critical operations (e.g., ACC, emergency braking).
        - **Redundancy**: Critical functions (e.g., **actuation**) are implemented with **fail-safes** (e.g., default to safe state on communication loss).
        - **Validation**: The **ACC control model** is validated against **ISO 15622** to ensure compliance with safety standards.
    - **Cybersecurity Measures**:
        - **Authentication**: Implied for **remote management** (e.g., only authorized users can send commands via the **Control Panel**).
        - **Data encryption**: Not explicitly mentioned, but **Wi-Fi communication** between entities should use **WPA3** or equivalent for secure data transmission.
        - **Access control**: **Role-based access** in the **User Entity** restricts operations to authorized personnel.
    - **Operational Safety**:
        - **Human-in-the-Loop**: The system is classified as a **Fully Autonomous Digital Twin with Human-in-the-Loop** (per **David and Bork taxonomy**), allowing operators to override automatic decisions (e.g., disabling ACC).
        - **Emergency protocols**: The **Control Panel** includes an **emergency braking** command to halt the vehicle in unsafe conditions.
    - **Fault Tolerance**:
        - **Sensor fault detection**: Anomalies in **LiDAR/camera data** trigger alerts or fallback modes (e.g., switching to a simpler tracking algorithm).
        - **Actuation fault tolerance**: The **Actuation FE** includes checks for **motor/servo failures** and defaults to a safe state.
    - **Compliance**:
        - **Functional Safety**: Implied for **actuation commands** to prevent accidents (e.g., unintended braking).
        - **Cybersecurity**: Likely adheres to **ISO/SAE 21434** for automotive cybersecurity, though not explicitly detailed in the case study.
    - **Remote Operation Risks**:
        - **Latency-induced hazards**: Mitigated by the **100 ms latency requirement** and **clock synchronization**.
        - **Unauthorized access**: Prevented via **authentication** and **access control** in the **User Entity**.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures: A Case Study

� �
Stefan Ramdhan, Winnie Trandinh [, Istvan David, Vera Pantelic, Mark Lawford](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4870-8433)
McMaster Centre for Software Certification (McSCert), Hamilton, Canada
McMaster University, Hamilton, Canada



_**Abstract**_ **—Digital twin (DT) technology has become of interest**
**in the automotive industry. There is a growing need for smarter**
**services that utilize the unique capabilities of DTs, ranging from**
**computer-aided remote control to cloud-based fleet coordination.**
**Developing such services starts with the software architecture.**
**However, the scarcity of DT architectural guidelines poses a**
**challenge for engineering automotive DTs. Currently, the only**
**DT architectural standard is the one defined in ISO 23247.**
**Though not developed for automotive systems, it is one of the**
**few feasible starting points for automotive DTs. In this work, we**
**investigate the suitability of the ISO 23247 reference architecture**
**for developing automotive DTs. Through the case study of**
**developing an Adaptive Cruise Control DT for a 1/10** **[th]** **-scale**
**autonomous vehicle, we identify some strengths and limitations**
**of the reference architecture and begin distilling future directions**
**for researchers, practitioners, and standard developers.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—architecture, automotive, case study, ISO 23247**


I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital twins (DT) are real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections [1] of real assets [2], offering cost-efficient
and safe alternatives for interacting with the asset and controlling it [3]. Digital twinning has been a transformative paradigm
in the engineering of complex cyber-physical systems [4],
many of which are safety-critical, such as manufacturing
systems [5], power grids [6], and biophysical systems [7]. The
success of DTs in such safety-critical domains made the case
for DTs in the automotive industry too [8], marked by an
increased interest in automotive DTs [9].
The complexity of DTs warrants careful architectural design. Unfortunately, the relative scarcity of architectural standards and reference architectures (RAs) for DTs hinder their
design, development, and by extension, their adoption. This
is a particularly acute problem in heavily standardized domains, such as automotive. Currently, the only DT-specific
architectural standard is the relatively new ISO 23247 –
Digital Twin Manufacturing Framework [10]. Published in
2021 by the International Organization for Standardization
(ISO), the standard defines a development framework for
digital twins, particularly in the manufacturing domain. Part 2
of the standard provides a detailed, layered RA designed and
developed for manufacturing systems. The other few available
DT standards are rather high-level and conceptual, and do
not define a RA—e.g., ISO/IEC 30173:2023 (Digital twin
— Concepts and terminology) [11]. As a consequence, DTs


�
S. Ramdhan and W. Trandinh contributed to the paper equally.



outside manufacturing are currently architected through standards that offer solutions to sub-problems of DT engineering.
Such limitations have been reported in an array of domains,
e.g., industrial IoT [12] and systems of systems [13]. In the
absence of better-suited alternatives, the ISO 23247 RA has
drawn increasing interest, e.g., in edge computing [14] and
AI simulation [15], and its adaptation for some domains has
already begun, e.g., in aerospace [16] and battery systems [17].
In this paper, we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247
RA for automotive DTs. Through a case study of a DT for
the adaptive cruise control (ACC) system in a 1/10 [th] -scale
autonomous vehicle, we identify benefits and limitations of
the RA, and open a discussion about standardized automotive
DT architectures. To facilitate reuse, we make our research
artifacts (source code and documentation) publicly available. [1]

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)


(UA) of IEC 62541 to facilitate the implementation of the Data
Translation FE, or implement a custom communication layer.
Although the standard was developed for the manufacturing
domain, its RA is an appealing feature for DT developers in
other domains. Shtofenmakher and Shao [16] adapt the RA
of ISO 23247 to space debris detection. In their approach,
the monolithic User Interface FE is decomposed into four
distinct FEs, each providing a specific viewpoint for space
data systems, public users, and satellite owners and operators,
who need to interact with the DT in very different ways. Since
not all space objects are controllable, the OME is decomposed
into controllable and uncontrollable Resident Space Objects.
Both types of OMEs are able to provide data through the
Data Collection and Control Entity. Cederbladh et al. [17]
investigate the fitness of the RA for DTs of heterogeneous
battery systems in electric vehicles. In their proposal, the DT
Entity is split into two components to execute on separate
hardware. A lightweight real-time component deals with timesensitive computation and an offline component deals with
computation-intensive tasks. Liu and David [15] investigate the
fitness of the RA for AI simulation, the technique of training
AI agents through simulated data. The proliferation of DTs has
had a positive impact on simulator technology too: today, some
of the most advanced simulators are found in DTs, making an
appealing case for implementing AI simulation by DTs. In
addition, DTs can collect data from the physical twin through
purposeful experimentation [18]. The _Collection Identification_
_FE_ is an apt spot to implement such features, while AI agents
can integrate with the DT through the User Entity.
These works underscore the utility of the ISO 23247 RA
outside manufacturing. Of course, part of this utility is due
to the lack of alternatives. Nonetheless, by properly mapping
domain-specific requirements, the RA can serve as a useful
starting point in the development of DTs outside of manufacturing. Our work is the first attempt at investigating the fitness
of the ISO 23247 RA for automotive systems.


_B. Automotive digital twins_


In the automotive context, the _ego vehicle_ refers to the
vehicle of interest hosting hardware and software that enable
autonomous driving. Streams of data from LiDAR, cameras,
and other sensors are fused in real time to detect objects in the
ego vehicle’s environment. Each object is typically assigned
an ID and is characterized by its state estimate—e.g., size,
position, velocity—which is updated every perception cycle,
and is called an _object track_ . Using these tracks, ACC can
be developed, which is an advanced driver assistance system
(ADAS) that adjusts the ego vehicle’s speed to maintain a
safe following distance behind the vehicle in its driving path,
called the _lead vehicle_ . The driver can set a _desired speed_ to
be maintained when no lead vehicle is present.
DTs can also be used to streamline the development of
vehicles and ADAS systems, or improve their operation [19].
During the design phase of automotive systems, DTs are often
used as cyber-physical experimentation facilities, thanks to
their advanced simulator components and the tight coupling



between the virtual and physical environments [20]. This is
often achieved in combination with 3D virtual technology, e.g.,
3D CAD design [21]. DTs are also used to validate compliance
with requirements [22]. During vehicle operation, DTs are
used for a variety of optimization and health monitoring
purposes. Ramtilak et al. [23] use a DT of spark ignition
to improve fuel economy and driving performance. Magargle
et al. [24] use DTs to collect vehicle data and analyze
vehicle performance to inform drivers. Rajesh et al. [25]
implement predictive maintenance of a brake system using a
DT, integrating a state-of-the-art simulator, CREO Simulate,
into it. Venkatesan et al. [26] implement a DT for health
monitoring and prognosis of a permanent magnet synchronous
motor. DTs are also used as a product lifecycle management
tool in support of digital threads of vehicles, establishing
a continuous, end-to-end information flow throughout the
lifecycle [27]. Additional insights into the role and applications
of DTs in automotive are available in the surveys of Deng et al.

[9] and Schwarz and Wang [28].


III. T HE A DAPTIVE C RUISE C ONTROL C ASE S TUDY


The goal of our work is to investigate the fitness of the ISO
23247 RA [10] for developing automotive DTs. To achieve
this goal, we choose the case study empirical genre, which
is “ _an empirical inquiry that investigates a contemporary_
_phenomenon (the case) in depth and within its real-world_
_context, especially when the boundaries between phenomenon_
_and context are unclear_ ” [29]. As such, case studies provide
particularly detailed and credible evidence of the investigated
phenomenon. Specifically, we conduct an evaluative case
study by developing the DT for the ACC of a 1/10 [th] -scale
autonomous vehicle, then assess the benefits and limitations
of the ISO 23247 architecture in a real-world setting.


_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

The physical twin (PT) is a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle
(Fig. 1), built off a Traxxas teleoperated vehicle [3] by retrofitting
it with autonomy components. In the retrofitting, we follow the
process provided by the open-source RoboRacer project [31].
As shown in Fig. 1b, the physical architecture consists of three
layers. The _lower level chassis_ is responsible for controlling
the steering and the speed of the vehicle. It is a strippeddown version of the off-the-shelf Traxxas Slash 4x4 Premium

vehicle. The _upper level chassis_ is mounted on top of the
lower level chassis to provide a physical platform for mounting
the technology required for enabling autonomous control and
digital twinning. The list of these autonomy components
include an _NVIDIA Jetson NX antenna_ and _NVIDIA Jetson_

_Orin Nano_ microcomputer, and onboard sensors including a


3 [https://traxxas.com/](https://traxxas.com/)



_Hokuyo UST-10LX_ LiDAR, an _Intel Deepsense D435i_ Depth
Camera, and a _Vedder Electronic Speed Controller_ .
We use _NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0_ as the Software Development
Kit (SDK) for programming the Jetson Orin Nano, and _Robot_
_Operating System 2_ (ROS2) [32] as the middleware to integrate
application modules. Application modules are implemented in
Python 3.8 (rapid prototyping), C++ (ROS2 node lifecycle
management), and MATLAB/Simulink (for more complex
systems, e.g., point cloud clustering and tracking).


IV. E NGINEERING A DT FOR A DAPTIVE C RUISE C ONTROL


We now provide a detailed exposition of the case study,
starting from the definition of DTS requirements (Sec. IV-A).
We then elaborate on the architectural design (Sec. IV-B) and
the implementation of DTS services (Sec. IV-C).


_A. DTS Requirements_


To drive its development, we first define system requirements for the DTS. We rely on a case-based generalization [33] from real projects – an approach that is particularly
effective when constructing middle-range theories that balance
generality with practicality, such as the ones in engineering
sciences. We draw on our expertise with automotive systems
and our ongoing projects in the area as we define the following
representative requirements for the DT.


**R1. The DTS shall use a standardized communication**

**interface between all components of the system.**
Ensures alignment with good system design principles,
such as modularity and loose coupling, to hide internal
complexities of each component.


**R2. The DTS shall collect real-time data about the ego**
**vehicle’s state.** The DTS shall collect the following ACCrelevant data in real-time:


   - ego vehicle’s speed;

   - ego vehicle’s steering angle;

   - detected objects in the vehicle’s proximity.


**R3. The DTS shall store the ego vehicle state data on a**
**device external to the vehicle.** Due to large quantities

**SW Component** **Description** **ISO 23247 FE**


**Physical Twin Entity**
Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter. Data Pre-Processing FE
Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands. Actuation FE


**Digital Twin Entity**
Visualization Pre-Processing Converts data into a format that the 3D Visualization can process. Presentation FE
Data Collection Collects object tracks and ego vehicle state. Data Collecting FE
Report Generation Generates human-readable reports of object tracks and vehicle state as a timeseries. Reporting FE
Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database. _N/A_

**User Entity**

of real-time data and limited storage on the vehicle, data
must be stored externally to enable data monitoring.


**R4. The DTS shall report real-time ego vehicle informa-**
**tion through a UI.** Drivers are more likely to trust ADAS
systems with transparent human-machine interfaces that
report relevant information of the ADAS decision-making
process [34]. Thus, the UI shall display the following
information about the ego vehicle in real-time:

   - ego vehicle’s speed;

   - ego vehicle’s steering angle;

   - tracks surrounding the ego vehicle;

   - projected driving path of the ego vehicle.


**R5. The DTS shall include a user interface to remotely**
**manage the ACC sub-system.** To enable remote management, the UI implements the following commands:

   - enable and disable ACC;

   - modify the desired speed of ACC;

   - perform emergency braking of the ego vehicle.


**R6. The DTS shall actuate the ego vehicle in response**
**to user commands and ACC control.** The system
shall implement ACC, as outlined in ISO 15622 [35].
The basic control strategy and functionality, described in

[35, Sec 6.1–6.2] shall be implemented for performance
class I ACC (no road curvature capabilities), while the
remaining requirements are not considered due to ACC
being implemented on a 1/10 [th] -scale vehicle.


**R7. The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most**
**100 ms from the vehicle to the DT Entity.** To enable
computation offloading for a real-time system, low endto-end latency is a must. This figure comes from the 3rd
Generation Partnership Plan (3GPP) Technical Specification, 22.185 [36], a specification of service requirements
for Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) communication.


**R8. Components within the DTS shall have clocks syn-**
**chronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.** Clock synchronization is especially critical in distributed systems
for the purposes of event ordering, data integrity, and



coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

_C. Implementation of Services_


Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of
multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II.
All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation
FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and
performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system.
Communication between SW components takes place within a
ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over
a Wi-Fi network using U
